Paging,segmentati
on

Paging
•Paging is a storage mechanism used to retrieve 
processes from the secondary storage into main 
memory in the form of pages.
•Main idea behind the paging is to divide each 
process in the form of pages. 
•Main memory will also be divided in form of frames.
•One page of the process is to be stored in one of the 
frames of the memory. 
•The pages can be stored at the different locations of 
the memory but the priority is always to find the 
contiguous frames or holes.

Paging
•Paging is fixed size partitioning scheme.
•In paging, secondary memory and main memory 
are divided into equal fixed size partitioning.
•Divide physical memory into fixed-sized blocks 
called frames
•Divide logical memory into blocks of same size 
called pages
•Set up a page table to translate logical to physical 
addresses.
•The size of the last part may be less than the page 
size.

Translating logical address into physical address
•CPU always generates a logical address.
•A physical address is needed to access the main 
memory.
 Step 1: 
 CPU generates a logical address consisting of two 
parts-
1. Page Number.
2. Page Offset.
–Page Number specifies the Specific Page of the 
process from which CPU wants to read the data.
–Page Offset specifies the Specific Word on the page 
that CPU wants to read.

Translating logical address into physical address
Step 2:
 For the page number generated by the CPU, Page 
Table provides the corresponding frames number (base 
address of the frame) where that page is stored in the 
main memory.

Translating logical address into physical address
Step 3:
• Physical address consisting of two parts-
1. Frame Number.
2. Page Offset.
•The Frame Number combined with the page offset 
forms the required physical address.
•Frame Number specifies the Specific Frame where 
the required page is stored.
•Page Offset specifies the Specific Word that has to be 
read from that page.

Translating logical address into physical address

Paging Model of Logical and  Physical Memory

Paging Example 
•Logical address:  n = 2 and  m = 4. 
•Using a page size of 4 bytes and 
•Physical memory of 32 bytes (8 pages)

Implementation of Page Table
•Page table is kept in main memory
–Page-table base register (PTBR) points to the page 
table
–Page-table length register (PTLR) indicates size of the 
page table
•In this scheme every data/instruction access 
requires two memory accesses
–One for the page table and one for the data / 
instruction
•The two-memory access problem can be solved by 
the use of a special fast-lookup hardware cache 
called  translation look-aside buffers (TLBs) (also 
called associative memory).

Segmentation
•Segmentation is another way of dividing the 
addressable memory.
•Logical address space is basically the collection of 
segments.
•Each segment has a name and a length.
•In OS, segmentation is a memory management 
technique in which the memory is divided into the 
variable size(segments) like Paging.
•Each part known as a segment which can be allocated 
to a process.
•Each segment are stored in a table called a segment 
table.

Segmentation(cont)
Example:- 
       A program is a collection of segments
–A segment is a logical unit such as:
❑ Main Program
❑ Procedure 
❑ Function
❑ Method
❑ Object
❑ Local Variables, Global Variables
❑ Common block
❑ Stack
❑ Symbol table
❑ Arrays

Segmentation Hardware(cont)
•Logical address consist of two tuple:
<segment-number, offset>
•Segment table - map two-dimensional physical 
addresses; each table entry has:
–Base- contains the starting physical address where the 
segments reside in memory.
–Limit- specifies the lengh of the segment.
•Segment-table base register (STBR) points to the 
segment table’s location in memory.
•Segment-table length register (STLR) indicates  number 
of segments used by a program.
•Segment number s is legal if s < STLR.

Segmentation Hardware

Virtual memory 
Virtual memory – separation of user logical 
memory from physical memory
–Only part of the program needs to be in memory 
for execution
–Logical address space can be much larger than 
physical address space
–Allows address spaces to be shared by several 
processes
–Allows for more efficient process creation
–More programs running concurrently
–Less I/O needed to load or swap processes

Virtual memory  (Cont.)
•Virtual address space – logical view of how 
process is stored in memory
–Usually start at address 0, contiguous addresses 
until end of space
–Meanwhile, physical memory organized in page 
frames
–MMU must map logical to physical
•Virtual memory can be implemented via:
–Demand paging 
–Demand segmentation

Virtual Memory is Larger than Physical Memory

Virtual-address Space
▪Logical address space for stack to start at Max 
logical address and grow “down” while heap grows 
“up”
•Maximizes address space use
•Unused address space between the two is 
hole
No physical memory needed until heap 
or stack grows to a given new page
▪Enables sparse address spaces with holes left for 
growth, dynamically linked libraries, etc.
▪System libraries shared via mapping into virtual 
address space
▪Shared memory by mapping pages read-write into 
virtual address space
▪Pages can be shared during fork(), speeding 
process creation

Shared Library Using Virtual Memory

Demand Paging
•Could bring entire process into memory at load time
    or bring a page into memory only when it is needed
–Less I/O needed, 
–no unnecessary I/O
–Less memory needed 
–Faster response
–More users
•Similar to paging system with swapping.
•Page is needed  reference to it
–invalid reference  abort
–not-in-memory  bring to memory
•Lazy swapper – never swaps a page into memory 
unless page will be needed
–Swapper that deals with pages is a pager

Basic Concepts 
•With swapping, pager guesses which pages will be 
used before swapping out again Instead, pager 
brings in only those pages into memory.
•How to determine that set of pages?
–Need new MMU functionality to implement demand 
paging
•If pages needed are already memory resident
–No difference from non-demand-paging
•If page needed and not memory resident
–Need to detect and load the page into memory from 
storage
•Without changing program behavior
•Without programmer needing to change code

Demand Paging Structure

Valid-Invalid Bit
•With each page table entry a valid–
invalid bit is associated 
    (v  in-memory – memory       
resident, i  not-in-memory)
•Initially  valid–invalid bit is set to i 
on all entries
•Example of a page table snapshot:
During MMU address translation,
     if valid–invalid bit in page table 
entry is i  page fault

Page Table When Some Pages Are Not
in Main Memory

Steps in Handling Page Fault
1.If there is a reference to a page, first reference to that 
page will trap to operating system 
–Page fault
2.Operating system looks at another table to decide:
–Invalid reference  abort
–Just not in memory
3.Find free frame
4.Swap page into frame via scheduled disk operation
5.Reset tables to indicate page now in memory
Set validation bit = v
6.Restart the instruction that caused the page fault

Steps in Handling a Page Fault (Cont.)

Page Replacement
•Prevent over-allocation of memory by 
modifying page-fault service routine to 
include page replacement
•Use modify (dirty) bit to reduce overhead of 
page transfers – only modified pages are 
written to disk
•Page replacement completes separation 
between logical memory and physical 
memory – large virtual memory can be 
provided on a smaller physical memory

Need For Page Replacement

Basic Page Replacement
1.Find the location of the desired page on disk
2.Find a free frame:
   -  If there is a free frame, use it
   -  If there is no free frame, use a page               
replacement algorithm to select a victim frame
   -  Write victim frame to disk if dirty
3.Bring  the desired page into the (newly) free 
frame; update the page and frame tables
4.Continue the process by restarting the 
instruction that caused the trap

Page Replacement Architecture

Page and Frame Replacement Algorithms
•Frame-allocation algorithm determines 
–How many frames to give each process
–Which frames to replace
•Page-replacement algorithm
–Want lowest page-fault rate on both first access and re-access
•Evaluate algorithm by running it on a particular string of 
memory references (reference string) and computing the 
number of page faults on that string
–String is just page numbers, not full addresses
–Repeated access to the same page does not cause a page fault
–Results depend on number of frames available
•In all our examples, the reference string of referenced 
page numbers is 
                7,0,1,2,0,3,0,4,2,3,0,3,0,3,2,1,2,0,1,7,0,1

There are 6 Page Replacement Techniques:
1.First-In-First-Out (FIFO) Algorithm
2.Optimal Algorithm
3.Least Recently Used (LRU) Algorithm
4.LRU Approximation Algorithms
5.Counting Based Algorithms
6.Page Buffering Algorithm

1. First-In-First-Out (FIFO) Algorithm
•Reference string:         
7,0,1,2,0,3,0,4,2,3,0,3,0,3,2,1,2,0,1,7,0,1
•3 frames (3 pages can be in memory at a time per 
process)
•Can vary by reference string: consider 
1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5
–Adding more frames can cause more page faults!
•Belady’s Anomaly
•How to track ages of pages? 
–Just use a FIFO queue
15 page faults

2. Optimal Algorithm
•Replace page that will not be used for (future) 
longest period of time
–9 is optimal for the example
•How do you know this?
–Can’t read the future
•Used for measuring how well your algorithm performs

3.Least Recently Used (LRU) Algorithm
•Use past knowledge rather than future(must be replace 
the LRU element)
•Replace page that has not been used in the most amount 
of time
•Associate time of last use with each page
•12 faults – better than FIFO but worse than OPT
•Generally good algorithm and frequently used
•But how to implement?

LRU Algorithm (Cont.)
•LRU and OPT are cases of stack algorithms 
that don’t have Belady’s Anomaly
•Use Of A Stack to Record Most Recent Page 
References

4.LRU Approximation Algorithms
•LRU needs special hardware and still slow
•Reference bit
–With each page associate a bit, initially = 0
–When page is referenced bit set to 1
–Replace any with reference bit = 0 (if one 
exists)
•We do not know the order, however

LRU Approximation Algorithms (cont.)
•Second-chance algorithm
–Generally FIFO, plus hardware-provided 
reference bit
–Clock replacement
–If page to be replaced has 
•Reference bit = 0 -> replace it
•reference bit = 1 then:
–set reference bit 0, leave page in memory
–replace next page, subject to same rules

Second-chance Algorithm

Enhanced Second-Chance Algorithm
•Improve algorithm by using reference bit and modify bit 
(if available) in concert
•Take ordered pair (reference, modify):
–(0, 0) neither recently used not modified – best page to 
replace
–(0, 1) not recently used but modified – not quite as good, 
must write out before replacement
–(1, 0) recently used but clean – probably will be used again 
soon
–(1, 1) recently used and modified – probably will be used 
again soon and need to write out before replacement
•When page replacement called for, use the clock 
scheme  but use the four classes replace page in lowest 
non-empty class
–Might need to search circular queue several times

5. Counting Algorithms
•Keep a counter of the number of references that 
have been made to each page
–Not common
•Lease Frequently Used (LFU) Algorithm:
–  Replaces page with smallest count
•Most Frequently Used (MFU) Algorithm: 
–Based on the argument that the page with the 
smallest count was probably just brought in and 
has yet to be used

6.Page-Buffering Algorithms
•Keep a pool of free frames, always
–Then frame available when needed, not found at fault time
–Read page into free frame and select victim to evict and 
add to free pool
–When convenient, evict victim
•Possibly, keep list of modified pages
–When backing store otherwise idle, write pages there and 
set to non-dirty
•Possibly, keep free frame contents intact and note 
what is in them
–If referenced again before reused, no need to load contents 
again from disk
–Generally useful to reduce penalty if wrong victim frame 
selected

Directory Structure 
•Adirectoryis a container that is used to contain 
folders and files. It organizes files and folders in a 
hierarchical manner.
•The operating system uses directories to track where 
files are stored, just like using folders to organize 
papers.
•Different directory structures can be used to suit 
various organizational needs.
•Understanding directory structures helps in 
organizing and accessing files more easily.

Different Types of Directories in OS
1.Single-Level Directory
2.Two-Level Directory
3.Tree Structure/ Hierarchical Structure
4.Acyclic Graph Structure
5.General-Graph Directory Structure

1) Single-Level Directory
•The single-level directory is thesimplest 
directory structure. In it, all files are contained 
in the same directory which makes it easy to 
support and understand.

•Since it is a single directory, so its implementation 
is very easy.
•If the files are smaller in size, searching will 
become faster.
•The operations like file creation, searching, 
deletion, updating are very easy in such a 
directory structure.
Advantages
•Logical Organization
•Increased Efficiency
•Improved Security
•Facilitates Backup and Recovery
•Scalability

2) Two-Level Directory
•each user has their ownuser files directory (UFD).
•The UFDs have similar structures, but each lists only 
the files of a single user. 
•System'smaster file directory (MFD)is searched 
whenever a new user id is created.

a single level directory often leads to confusion 
of files names among different users. The 
solution to this problem is to create aseparate 
directory for each user.

3)Tree Structure/ Hierarchical Structure
•It used in ourpersonal computers. User can 
create files and subdirectories too, which was 
a disadvantage in the previous directory 
structures.
•This directory structure resembles a real tree 
upside down, where theroot directoryis at 
the peak. 
•This root contains all the directories for each 
user. The users can create subdirectories and 
even store files in their directory.



Advantages
•This directory structure allows subdirectories 
inside a directory.
•The searching is easier.
•File sorting of important and unimportant 
becomes easier.
•This directory is more scalable than the other 
two directory structures explained.

4) Acyclic Graph Structure
•AGS, where a file in one directory can be 
accessed from multiple directories. 
•These files could be shared in between the 
users. 
•It is designed in a way that multiple directories 
point to a particular directory or file with the 
help of links.



5) General-Graph Directory Structure
•Unlike the acyclic-graph directory, which avoids 
loops, the general-graph directory can have cycles, 
meaning a directory can contain paths that loop 
back to the starting point. 
•This can make navigating and managing files more 
complex.
Advantages of General-Graph Directory
•More flexible than other directory structures.
•Allows cycles, meaning directories can loop back to 
each other.

General-Graph Directory Structure

File-System Structure
•File structure
–Logical storage unit
–Collection of related information
•File system resides on secondary storage (disks)
–Provided user interface to storage, mapping logical to physical
–Provides efficient and convenient access to disk by allowing 
data to be stored, located retrieved easily
•Disk provides in-place rewrite and random access
–I/O transfers performed in blocks of sectors (usually 512 
bytes)
•File control block (FCB) – storage structure consisting of 
information about a file
•Device driver controls the physical device 
•File system organized into layers

Layered File System

Allocation Method

–Contiguous

Contiguous Allocation Method

–Best performance in most cases

Free-Space Management (Cont.)

•Counting

Free-Space Management (Cont.)
•Space Maps
–Used in ZFS
–Consider meta-data I/O on very large file systems
•Full data structures like bit maps cannot fit in memory ➔ 
thousands of I/Os
–Divides device space into metaslab units and manages

–Each metaslab has associated space map
•Uses counting algorithm
–But records to log file rather than file system

RAID Levels
•RAID (Redundant Array of Independent) is a method 
of combining multiple physical disks into a single 
logical storage unit.
•Purpose:
–Improve performance (faster read/write).
–Provide fault tolerance (data recovery in case of disk 
failure).
–Increase storage capacity.
•Implemented in:
–Hardware (RAID controllers).
•Software (OS-level RAID).

RAID Levels

RAID Levels
RAID 0: Striping
•Data is split (striped) across multiple disks.
•No redundancy (if one disk fails → all data lost).
•Performance: High read/write speed.
•Fault Tolerance: None.
•Storage Efficiency: 100%.
Use case: Applications needing high performance but 
not critical (e.g., gaming, video editing).

RAID 1: Mirroring
•Data is duplicated (mirrored) across two or more 
disks.
•If one disk fails, data is still safe on the other.
•Performance: Faster reads (read from any disk), 
writes are slower (data written to both).
•Fault Tolerance:  High (survives single disk failure).
•Storage Efficiency: 50% (half space used for 
redundancy).
Use case: Critical data storage (banking, medical 
records).

RAID 2: Bit-level Striping with Hamming Code
•Data striped at bit level with error-correcting codes 
(ECC).
•Rarely used in practice (too complex, requires many 
disks).
•Performance: Moderate.
•Fault Tolerance: Good (ECC recovers errors).
Use case: Theoretical interest, not common in real 
systems.

RAID 3: Byte-level Striping with Dedicated Parity
•Data striped at byte level across disks.
•One disk stores parity information.
•If one disk fails, parity is used for recovery.
•Performance: Good for sequential access.
•Fault Tolerance:  Yes, but parity disk is a 
bottleneck.
• Use case: Applications with large sequential 
reads/writes (multimedia)

RAID 4: Block-level Striping with Dedicated Parity
•Data striped at block level (not bytes).
•One disk holds parity.
•Faster random reads than RAID 3, but parity disk still 
a bottleneck.
•Performance: Good reads, slower writes.
•Fault Tolerance:  Yes.
Use case: Rarely used due to parity bottleneck

RAID 5: Block-level Striping with Distributed Parity
•Data and parity are distributed across all disks.
•No single parity disk → avoids bottleneck.
•Performance: Good read speed, better write 
speed than RAID 4.
•Fault Tolerance:  Yes (can survive one disk 
failure).
•Storage Efficiency: (N-1)/N, where N = number of 
disks.
Use case: Most popular RAID – used in servers, 
databases, and file systems

RAID 6: Block-level Striping with Double Distributed 
Parity
•Similar to RAID 5, but stores two sets of parity.
•Can survive two disk failures.
•Performance: Read speed good, write speed slower 
(due to extra parity calculations).
•Storage Efficiency: (N-2)/N.
Use case: Mission-critical systems needing high 
reliability (enterprise servers)

RAID 10 (1+0): Combination of RAID 1 & RAID 0
•Data is mirrored (RAID 1) and then striped (RAID 0).
•Provides both high performance and fault tolerance.
•Fault Tolerance:  Yes (can survive multiple failures, 
as long as not both in same mirror pair).
•Storage Efficiency: 50%.
Use case: High-performance databases, financial 
systems.



Disk Scheduling Algorithm
•A Disk Scheduling Algorithm is a method used 
by the operating system to decide the order in 
which pending disk I/O requests (read or 
write operations) are served.
•Disk’s read/write head must move (seek) to 
different tracks to access data, multiple 
requests may arrive simultaneously. 
•Without scheduling, the head may move 
inefficiently, causing high seek time and 
latency.

Goal of disk scheduling algorithms is
•Minimize seek time (time to move the head 
to the required track)
•Reduce rotational latency (waiting for the 
right sector under the head)
•Improve throughput (number of I/O requests 
served per unit time)
•Provide fairness among all requests



























Swap-Space Management
•Used for moving entire processes (swapping), or pages 
(paging), from DRAM to secondary storage when 
DRAM not large enough for all processes
•Operating system provides swap space management
–Secondary storage slower than DRAM, so important to 
optimize performance
–Usually multiple swap spaces possible – decreasing I/O 
load on any given device
–Best to have dedicated devices
–Can be in raw partition or a file within a file system (for 
convenience of adding)
–Data structures for swapping on Linux systems:

Swap-Space Management

Stable Storage in OS
•Stable storage is a form of data storage that survives 
system crashes and failures without losing data.
•It ensures that once data is written, it remains intact 
even after hardware/software failures.
Purpose:
•Provides a reliable way to store critical system 
information.
•Used in situations where data consistency and 
durability are essential (e.g., databases, transaction 
systems).

Techniques to Achieve Stable Storage:
•Redundancy
–Data is stored in multiple locations.
–Even if one copy is corrupted, the other can be recovered.
•Mirroring (Disk Duplication)
–Each write is made on two disks.
–If one disk fails, the other holds the data.
•Write-ahead Logging (WAL)
–Before modifying data, changes are written into a log.
–Ensures recovery to a consistent state after crashes.
•Error-Detection & Correction
–Techniques like checksums, parity bits, ECC codes are 
used.
–Detects corruption and corrects errors.

Tertiary Storage Structure in Operating System
•Tertiary storage is a low-cost, high-capacity storage 
that is not directly accessed by the CPU.
•It is slower compared to primary (RAM) and 
secondary storage (Hard disks, SSDs).
•Typically used for backup, archival, and large-scale 
data storage.
Characteristics:
•High capacity (can store terabytes or petabytes).
•Low cost per bit.
•Slow access speed (compared to RAM/Hard Disk).
•Usually removable or external storage media.

Examples of Tertiary Storage:
•Magnetic Tape Storage
–Used for backup and archival.
–Sequential access device (slow random access).
•Optical Discs (CDs, DVDs, Blu-ray)
–Removable, portable storage.
•Magnetic Disks / Removable Cartridges
–Sometimes used as tertiary storage.
•Cloud Storage (Modern Perspective)
–Functions as tertiary storage for large datasets.
Storage Hierarchy (Speed vs Capacity):
•Primary Storage (Fastest, Expensive, Small) → RAM, Cache
•Secondary Storage (Moderate Speed, Cheaper) → HDD, SSD
•Tertiary Storage (Slowest, Cheapest, Huge) → Tapes, 
Optical, Cloud

UNIT-1
 Operating Systems & System Programming
(23ACA06)
 

Operating System
•An operating system is an interface between a 
computer user and computer hardware. 
•An operating system is a software which performs all 
the basic tasks such as file management, memory 
mgmt, process mgmt, handling input and output, 
and controlling peripheral devices such as disk drives 
and printers.
•The software that contains the core components of 
the operating system is called thekernel.
2

Architecture of OS
3

Computer System Components
1.Hardware – provides basic computing resources (CPU, 
memory, I/O devices).
2. Operating system – controls and coordinates the use of the 
hardware among the various application programs for the 
various users.
3. Applications programs – The system resources are used to 
solve the computing problems of the users.
4. Users - (people, machines, other computers). Abstract View of 
System Components
4

Operating system goals
–Execute user programs and make solving user 
problems easier.
–Make the computer system convenient to use.
–Use the computer hardware in an efficient 
manner.
5

Functions of Operating System
i. Process Management
•A process is a program in execution. 
•A process needs certain resources, including CPU time, 
memory, files, and I/O devices, to accomplish its task.
The Following activities are, 
✦ Process creation and deletion.
✦ process suspension and resumption.
✦ Provision of mechanisms for:
–• process synchronization
–• process communication
6

ii. Main-Memory Management
•Memory is a large array of words or bytes, each with its 
own address. 
•It is a repository of quickly accessible data shared by the 
CPU and I/O devices.
•Main memory is a volatile storage device. 
•It loses its contents in the case of system failure.
•Following activities are, 
 Keep track of which parts of memory are currently 
being used and by whom.
 Decide which processes to load when memory space 
becomes available.
 Allocate and de-allocate memory space as needed.
7

iii. File Management
•A file is a collection of related information defined by its 
creator. Commonly, files represent programs and data.
Following activities are, 
✦ File creation and deletion.
✦ Directory creation and deletion.
✦Support of primitives for manipulating files and directories.
✦ Mapping files onto secondary storage.
✦ File backup on stable (non-volatile) storage media.
8

iv. I/O System Management
The I/O system consists of:
✦ A buffer-caching system
✦ A general device-driver interface
✦ Drivers for specific hardware devices
9

v. Secondary-Storage Management
•Main memory (primary storage) is volatile and too 
small to accommodate all data and programs 
permanently the computer system must provide 
secondary storage to back up main memory.
•the following activities are, 
✦ Free space management
✦ Storage allocation
✦ Disk scheduling
10

Types of Operating Systems
Operating systems can be classified into several types 
based on their functionality and usage.
•Batch, Time-Sharing, Distributed, 
•Network, 
•Real-time, Mobile, 
•Embedded operating systems.
Additionally, there are based on user count 
•single-user, 
•multi-user,
•processing capabilities (multiprogramming, 
multiprocessing, multitasking), and other factors.
11

1. Batch Operating System:
•Processes jobs in batches without direct user 
interaction.
•Suitable for tasks that can be completed offline, like 
payroll processing or generating reports.
2. Time-Sharing Operating System:
•Allows multiple users to access the system 
simultaneously, sharing resources like CPU time.
•Enables interactive computing, where users can run 
programs and receive feedback in real-time.
12

3. Distributed Operating System:
•Manages a collection of interconnected computers, 
presenting them as a single system.
•Facilitates resource sharing and communication 
between different machines.
4. Network Operating System:
•Runs on a server and manages network resources 
like files, printers, and users.
•Provides services for network communication, 
security, and administration.
13

5. Real-Time Operating System:
•Designed for systems where timing is critical, like 
industrial control or medical devices.
•Responds to events within a strict time constraint.
6. Mobile Operating System:
•Specifically designed for mobile devices like 
smartphones and tablets.
•Optimized for touch-based interfaces and mobile-
specific features.

efficiency.

development of network operating systems.

1.one-to one model

1. One-to-One Model
•This model maps each user thread to a kernel 
thread. 
•It provides more concurrency than many-to-one 
model by allowing another thread to run when a 
thread makes a blocking system call. 
•It allows multiple threads to run in parallel on 
multiprocessors. 
•Drawback: kernel threads may burden the 
performance of a system. 

2. Many-to-One Model
•This model maps many user-level threads to one 
kernel thread. 
•Thread management is done by the thread library in 
user space, so it is efficient. 
•However, the entire process will block if a thread 
makes a blocking system call.

3. Many-to-Many Model
•The many-to-many model (many user-level threads to 
many kernel-level threads) avoids many of the 
limitations of the one-to-one model while extending 
multithreading capabilities even further.
•In this model, a user-level threads library provides 
sophisticated scheduling of user-level threads above 
kernel threads.

Turnaround Time –

Waiting Time – 
•Time a process spends waiting in the ready queue. 
•Lower waiting time is desirable.
Response Time – 
•Amount of time it takes from when a request was 
submitted until the first response is produced.

Issues of critical section
1.Deadlock:
•When two or more threads or processes wait for 
each other to release a critical section, It can result 
in a deadlock situation.
•Deadlocks can be difficult to detect and resolve, and 
its significant impact on a program’s performance 
and reliability.

Issues of critical section
2. Starvation:
•When a thread or process is repeatedly prevented 
from entering a critical section, So, thread or 
process is unable to progress. 
•This can happen if the critical section is held for an 
unusually long period of time, or if a high-priority 
thread or process is always given priority when 
entering the critical section.

Issues of critical section
3. Overhead:
•Overhead may reduce the program’s overall 
performance.
•When using critical sections, threads or processes 
must acquire and release locks or semaphores, 
which can take time and resources.

time.
•Thus, the implementation becomes the critical section 
problem where the wait and signal code are placed in the 
critical section.



Deadlock:
Deadlock may occur when two or more  
processes try to get the same multiple 
resources at a same time.

Starvation:
•Starvation is a problem of resource management, the 
process does not have resources because it is being 
used by other processes. 
•This problem occurs mainly in apriority-based 
scheduling algorithmin which the requests with high 
priority get processed first and the low priority 
processes get blocked for indefinite time.

Semaphore Implementation with no Busy waiting 
•With each semaphore there is an associated waiting 
queue
Each entry in a waiting queue has two data items:
– Value ( integer)
– Pointer to next record in the list
Two operations:
–block – place the process invoking the operation on 
the appropriate waiting queue
–wakeup – remove one of processes in the waiting 
queue and place it in the ready queue.

Semaphore Implementation with Busy waiting
                        

Implementation with no Busy waiting (Cont.)
wait(semaphore *S) { 
   S->value--; 
   if (S->value < 0) {
      add this process to S->list; 
      block(); 
   } 
}
signal(semaphore *S) { 
   S->value++; 
   if (S->value <= 0) {
      remove a process P from S->list; 
      wakeup(P); 
   } 
} 

Readers-Writers Problem
•A data set is shared among a number of concurrent 
processes.
–Readers : only read the data set; they do not perform 
any updates
–Writers   : can both read and write
•Problem – allow multiple readers to read at the same 
time.  
–Only one single writer can access the shared data 
at the same time.

Readers-Writers Problem (cont...)
•Shared Data
–Data set
–Semaphore mutex initialized to 1 (controls access to 
readcount)
–Semaphore wrt initialized to 1 (writer access)
–Integer readcount initialized to 0 (how many processes 
are reading object)

Readers-Writers Problem(cont..)
•The structure of a writer process 
              do {
                        wait (wrt) ;
                             //    writing is performed
                        signal (wrt) ;
             } while (TRUE);
       

Dining-Philosophers Problem
•Shared data 
–Bowl of rice (data set)
–Semaphore chopstick [5] initialized to 1

Dining-Philosophers Problem
• The K philosophers are seated around a circular table 
with one chopstick between each pair of philosophers. 
• There is one chopstick between each philosopher. 
• A philosopher may eat if he can pick up the two 
chopsticks adjacent to him.
• One chopstick may be picked up by any one of its 
adjacent followers but not both.

Dining-Philosophers Problem
•There are two states of the philosopher:THINKING 
and EATING. 
•Here two semaphores: Mutex and a semaphore array 
for the philosophers. 
•Mutex is used such that no two philosophers may 
access the pickup or put it down at the same time.
• Semaphore array is used to control the behavior of 
each philosopher. 
•But, semaphores can result in deadlock due to 
programming errors.

Dining-Philosophers Problem
Solution
•Mutual Exclusion Principle:No two Philosophers can have 
the two forks simultaneously.
•Free from Deadlock:Each philosopher can get the chance 
to eat in a certain finite time.
•Free from Starvation:When few Philosophers are waiting 
then one gets a chance to eat in a while.
•No strict Alternation
•Proper utilization of time

Deadlock
•Deadlockis a situation where a set of 
processes are blocked because each process is 
holding a resource and waiting for another 
resource acquired by some other process.
•A situation occurs in operating systems when 
there are two or more processes that hold 
some resources and wait for resources held by 
other(s).

Deadlock
For example,
•Process 1 is holding Resource 1 and waiting 
for resource 2 which is acquired by process 2, 
and process 2 is waiting for resource 1.

Deadlock Characterization
1. Necessary Condition
2. Resource -Allocation Graph

1. Necessary Condition
•Mutual exclusion:  only one thread at a time 
can use a resource( not sharable).
•Hold and wait:  a thread holding at least one 
resource is waiting to acquire additional 
resources held by other threads.
•No preemption:  a resource can be released 
only voluntarily by the thread holding it, after 
that thread has completed its task.
Deadlock can arise if four conditions hold simultaneously.

Graph with a Cycle But no Deadlock

Basic Facts
❑ If graph contains no cycles  no deadlock
❑ If graph contains a cycle 

–if several instances per resource type,

Methods for Handling Deadlocks
1.Ensure that the system will never enter a 
deadlock state:
–Deadlock prevention
–Deadlock avoidance
2.Allow the system to enter a deadlock 
state and then recover
3.Ignore the problem and pretend that 
deadlocks never occur in the system.

Deadlock Prevention
Deadlock prevention algorithms ensure that at 
least one of the necessary condition (Mutual 
Exclusion, Hold and wait, No preemption and 
circular wait) does not hold true.
Deadlock Prevention Deadlocks can be prevented 
by preventing at least one of the four required 
condition.

Deadlock Prevention
1.Mutual Exclusion: →  Sharable
     (more process trying to access single resource)
• Shared resources such as read only file do not 
lead to deadlocks.
•Unfortunately, some resources, such as printer and 
tap drives, require exclusive access by single 
process.

Deadlock Prevention (Cont.)
2. Hold and wait:
–Allocate all required resources to the process 
before the start of its execution this way Hold and 
Wait condition is eliminated.
Example: printer
–Allows process to request resources only when the 
process has None.
Disadvantages: 
–Low Resource Utilization
–Starvation

Deadlock Prevention (Cont.)

3. No Preemption: 
•if a process that is holding some resources 
requests another resource that cannot be 
immediately allocated to it, then all resources 
currently being held are Released.
•if a process requests resources, check if 
resource are available.
• if not available, allocated to some other 
process that is waiting (Forced).

Deadlock Prevention (Cont.)

4.Circular Wait:
➢ Assign numerical value to resources.
➢One way to avoid Circular Wait is to number

(increasing or decreasing) order.

Disk → 7
Scanner → 11

Deadlock Avoidance
Deadlock avoidance in OS isa strategy to prevent 
deadlocks by carefully managing resource allocation, 
ensuring the system always remains in a safe state.
Basic Facts
– If a system is in safe state  no deadlocks.
– If a system is in unsafe state  possibility of   
     deadlock.
– Avoidance  ensure that a system will never 
    enter an unsafe state.

Safe, Unsafe, Deadlock State 

Key Concepts in Banker's Algorithm
•Safe State:There exists at least one sequence of 
processes such that each process can obtain the 
needed resources, complete its execution, release 
its resources, and allow other processes to 
eventually complete without entering a deadlock.
•Unsafe State:Even though the system can still 
allocate resources to some processes, there is no 
guarantee that all processes can finish without 
potentially causing a deadlock.

DMA Mode Of Operations



Input/Output (I/O) Software
•I/O System: Manages all input and output operations 
between the computer and external devices (disk, 
printer, keyboard, display, etc.).
•I/O Software: A set of system software components 
that provide a framework for I/O operations.
•Goals:
–Provide abstraction (hide hardware details).
–Ensure efficiency (fast data transfer).
–Guarantee protection (controlled access).

Goals of I/O Software
•Device Independence → Applications should work 
with any device without modification.
•Uniform Naming → All devices accessed in a 
standard way (e.g., Linux treats devices as files).
•Error Handling → Consistent error reporting across 
devices.
•Buffering → Handle speed mismatch between CPU 
and devices.
•Concurrency → Support multiple processes 
accessing I/O simultaneously.
•Portability → Applications portable across systems.

Device-independent I/O Software
• I/O Software: Provides mechanisms for 
communication between CPU, memory, and 
peripheral devices.
•Device-independent I/O software abstracts 
hardware differences and offers a uniform 
interface for applications.
•Ensures portability, ease of programming, 
and system efficiency.

Goals of Device-Independent I/O Software
•Uniformity: Same operations for all devices (e.g., 
read, write).
•Portability: Applications do not depend on 
specific device hardware.
•Error Handling: Provides consistent error 
detection and reporting.
•Buffering: Smooths out speed differences 
between devices and CPU.
•Naming: Provides a consistent naming scheme 
(file names, device paths).
•Protection: Ensures controlled access to devices.

Functions of Device-Independent I/O Software
•Uniform Naming of Devices
–Devices are identified using symbolic names (e.g., 
/dev/disk1).
–Hides hardware-specific details like device numbers.
•Device Protection
–Restricts access to devices using permissions (read, 
write, execute).
–Prevents multiple processes from conflicting over 
device use.
•Blocking/Non-blocking Operations
–Blocking: Process waits until I/O completes.
–Non-blocking: Process continues execution, I/O 
happens in the background.

Goals of Device-Independent I/O Software
1. Buffering
–Temporary storage of data during transfer.
–Types:
•Single Buffering: One buffer between device 
and process.
•Double Buffering: Two buffers for overlapping 
computation and I/O.
•Circular Buffering: Multiple buffers arranged in 
a ring.

Goals of Device-Independent I/O Software
2. Error Handling
–Reports errors in a consistent way (timeouts, 
device failures).
–Allows retry or recovery without exposing 
device-specific details.
3. Device-Independent Access
–Provides system calls like open(), read(), 
write(), close() applicable to files, disks, or 
terminals.



Layers of I/O Software
(a) User-Level I/O Software
•Part of application programs.
•Uses standard libraries (e.g., printf, scanf, fopen, 
fread).
•Simplifies programmer’s work.
(b) Device-Independent I/O Software
–Provides uniform interface to all devices.
–Functions: Naming, protection, buffering, 
error reporting.

(c) Device Drivers
–Device-specific code in the OS kernel.
–Converts generic OS requests into device 
commands.
–Example: Printer driver, graphics driver.
(d) Interrupt Handlers
–Part of OS that responds to hardware 
interrupts.
–Alerts CPU when I/O operation completes.
–(e) Hardware Layer
–Actual I/O devices and controllers.
–Examples: Disk, keyboard, display, network 
card.

I/O Techniques
•Programmed I/O
–CPU actively waits and checks device status.
–Simple but wastes CPU time.
•Interrupt-Driven I/O
–Device interrupts CPU when ready.
–More efficient than polling.
•Direct Memory Access (DMA)
–Special controller transfers data between device 
and memory without CPU intervention.
–CPU is free for other tasks.

Device Drivers
•Device Driver: A software module that acts as 
an intermediary between the operating 
system and a hardware device.
•Purpose: To provide the OS with a standard 
interface to communicate with hardware 
without needing to know device-specific 
details.
•Example: Printer driver, graphics card driver, 
keyboard driver.

Role of Device Drivers
•Hardware Abstraction
–Hides device-specific details from the OS.
–Provides a uniform API for applications and the kernel.
•Command Translation
–Converts OS/system calls (like read, write) into device-
specific commands.
•Communication Management
–Transfers data between I/O devices and main memory.
•Interrupt Handling
–Handles device-generated interrupts and notifies the OS 
when tasks are completed.
•Error Detection & Handling
–Detects device errors and reports them in a standardized 
format.

Functions of Device Drivers
•Initialization: Configure device settings at 
startup.
•Device Control: Send commands to the 
device.
•Data Transfer: Move data to/from device and 
memory.
•Status Monitoring: Report current device 
status to OS.
•Synchronization: Manage concurrent access 
requests.

Types of Device Drivers
•Character Device Drivers
–Handle character-based I/O (one byte/character at a 
time).
–Examples: Keyboard, mouse, serial ports.
•Block Device Drivers
–Handle block-based data transfer (fixed-size blocks).
–Examples: Hard disk, SSD, CD-ROM.
•Virtual Device Drivers
–Do not correspond to physical devices; instead, 
emulate a device in software.
–Example: Virtual printers, virtual network adapters.

Device Driver Architecture in OS
•Application Programs → request I/O via 
system calls (open, read, write).
•OS I/O Subsystem → passes request to 
appropriate driver.
•Device Driver → translates request to device-
specific commands.
•Hardware Device → performs the actual 
operation and sends status back.

Interrupt Handlers
•When an I/O device needs attention (e.g., 
data is ready to be read), it sends an interrupt 
signal to the CPU, which triggers the 
appropriate interrupt handler to perform the 
necessary actions.
Hardware:
•This is the physical layer, including the actual 
devices and their controllers, which the device 
drivers interact with directly.

Security Environment 
•A Security Environment in OS defines the 
framework of policies, mechanisms, and tools that 
protect the system against threats.
•It provides a safe execution environment for 
processes, users, and applications by ensuring 
confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
1. Components of Security Environment
•The OS security environment consists of multiple 
layers:

❑User-Level Security
•Authentication: Verifies identity of users (passwords, 
biometrics, tokens).
•Authorization: Defines what an authenticated user can 
access (files, processes, devices).
•User Roles: Admin, normal users, and guest accounts 
with different privileges.
❑Process-Level Security
•Isolation: Processes are separated to prevent 
interference.
•Memory Protection: Prevents one process from 
accessing another process’s memory.
•Execution Modes: User Mode vs. Kernel Mode ensures 
limited access.

❑File and Data Security
•File Permissions: Read, Write, Execute (e.g., Unix 
rwx model).
•Encryption: Protects sensitive files and 
communications.
•Access Control Lists (ACLs): Define fine-grained 
access rights.
❑Network Security
•Firewalls: Prevent unauthorized external access.
•Intrusion Detection/Prevention: Detect and 
block malicious activities.
•Secure Protocols: HTTPS, SSH, VPNs for secure 
communication.

❑System Monitoring and Auditing
•Audit Logs: Record user and system activities.
•Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) and Antivirus.
•System Updates and Patches: Prevent exploitation of 
vulnerabilities

2. Security Environment Goals
•Confidentiality – Prevent unauthorized access 
to data.
•Integrity – Ensure data accuracy and prevent 
tampering.
•Availability – Guarantee system resources are 
accessible to authorized users.
•Accountability – Record user actions for 
auditing.
•Non-repudiation – Ensure users cannot deny 
their actions.

3. Threats to Security Environment
•Internal Threats: Privilege abuse, careless 
users.
•External Threats: Hackers, malware, phishing.
•System Threats: Worms, viruses, Trojan 
horses.
•Network Threats: Denial-of-Service (DoS), 
spoofing, packet sniffing

4. Mechanisms to Maintain Security Environment
•Authentication Mechanisms
–Passwords, biometrics, smart cards, multi-factor 
authentication.
•Access Control Models
–DAC (Discretionary Access Control)
–MAC (Mandatory Access Control)
–RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)
•Cryptography
–Data encryption (AES, RSA) and digital signatures.

Cont...
•System Hardening
–Disabling unused ports/services, applying patches, 
enforcing security policies.
•Monitoring & Auditing
–Logging, intrusion detection, and auditing for 
suspicious activities.

Security Design Principles 
Security Design Principles 
•The design of an OS to make it secure, 
reliable, and resistant to attacks.
•Its ensure that system resources are protected 
against unauthorized access, misuse, and 
failures.
•These principles were first formalized by 
Saltzer and Schroeder

Principle of Least Privilege
•Each process, program, or user should have 
only the minimum access rights needed to 
perform its task.
•Reduces damage in case of compromise.
Example: Normal users cannot install system 
software; only administrators can.

Principle of Fail-Safe Defaults
•Default state should deny access, and explicit 
permission must be given to allow access.
•Prevents accidental access to resources.
Example: File permissions in Linux default to 
restricted unless granted

Principle of Economy of Mechanism
•Security design should be kept simple and small.
•Easier to analyze, verify, and implement.
Example: Unix “rwx” permission model is simple 
and effective.
Principle of Complete Mediation
•Every access request should be checked for 
authorization.
•No caching or bypassing of security checks.
Example: Each file access in modern OS goes 
through the kernel’s access control checks

Principle of Open Design
•Security should not depend on secrecy of design or 
implementation, but on secrecy of keys/passwords.
•Promotes trust and allows peer review.
Example: OpenSSL is publicly available but secure 
because of cryptographic keys.
Principle of Separation of Privilege
•Access to critical resources should require multiple 
conditions or approvals.
•Reduces single point of failure.
Example: Two-factor authentication (password + OTP).

Principle of Least Common Mechanism
•Minimize sharing of resources among multiple 
users/processes.
•Prevents unintended information leakage.
Example: Separate user environments in multi-user 
OS.
Principle of Psychological Acceptability
•Security mechanisms should be easy to use and 
understand.
•If too complex, users may bypass or disable them.
Example: GUI-based security settings in Windows vs 
complex command-line settings

User Authentication
Authentication is the process of verifying the 
identity of a user before granting access to 
system resources.
•It ensures that only legitimate users can 
access files, applications, and system services.
•Plays a key role in OS security environment 
along with authorization and access control.

Goals of User Authentication
•Verify Identity – Confirm that the user is who 
they claim to be.
•Prevent Unauthorized Access – Block 
attackers or unauthorized users.
•Accountability – Record actions of 
authenticated users.
•Enable Authorization – Authentication is the 
first step before assigning permissions.

Methods of User Authentication
1. Password-Based Authentication
•Most common and simplest method.
•Weakness: Password guessing, brute force.
•Countermeasures: Strong pwds, hashing, pwd 
policies.
2. Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
•Uses two or more authentication factors for 
stronger security.
•Example: ATM access requires CARD + PIN.

Methods of User Authentication
3. Token-Based Authentication
•Uses hardware/software tokens that generate 
temporary codes (OTP).
•Example: RSA tokens, Google Authenticator.
4. Biometric Authentication
•Uses unique physical/behavioral traits.
•Examples: Fingerprint scanners, face unlock, retina 
scans.
•Weakness: Privacy issues, expensive, false 
acceptance/rejection rates.

5. Certificate-Based Authentication
•Uses digital certificates and public key 
cryptography.
•Common in secure networks, corporate systems.
6. Single Sign-On (SSO)
•User logs in once and gains access to multiple 
applications without re-authentication.
•Example: Google Account login across Gmail, 
Drive, YouTube.

Protection Mechanisms 
•In OS, protection mechanisms ensure that I/O 
devices and data are accessed securely and 
correctly.
•Protection is required to prevent unauthorized 
access, avoid data corruption, and provide 
controlled sharing among processes.
•Since I/O devices are shared resources, improper 
or malicious use can crash the system or 
compromise security.

Objectives I/O Protection
•Prevent unauthorized access to devices.
•Protect data integrity during read/write 
operations.
•Ensure controlled sharing among processes.
•Provide fault isolation so that one faulty 
process does not affect others.
•Enable safe execution of user programs by 
restricting direct access to hardware

Protection Mechanisms
(a) Dual Mode Operation
•CPU operates in two modes:
–User Mode: User programs run with limited 
privileges.
–Kernel (Supervisor) Mode: OS runs with full 
privileges.

(b) Access Control
•Controls which users/processes can access 
which devices.
•Achieved using:
–Access Control Lists (ACLs) → define permissions 
for each device.
–Capability Lists → specify what rights a process 
has.
•Prevents accidental or malicious misuse of 
devices.

(c) Memory Protection in I/O
•Ensures that I/O devices cannot overwrite 
memory of other processes.
•Achieved using:
–Base and Limit Registers → restricts memory 
access range.
–I/O Mapping → isolates I/O address space from 
main memory.

(d) I/O Privileged Instructions
•Certain I/O-related instructions (e.g., IN, OUT) 
are privileged.
•Executed only in kernel mode to avoid misuse.
•Example: A user program cannot directly send 
commands to disk controller

(e) Device Access Control
•OS uses Device Drivers to control access.
•Drivers implement:
–Queuing (to schedule multiple requests).
–Buffering (to prevent data loss).
–Error handling (to avoid system crashes).

(f) Interrupt Handling Protection
•Interrupts are used for device communication.
•OS ensures:
–Only authorized interrupt handlers run in kernel 
mode.
•Malicious code cannot replace handlers
(g) File System Protection
•Since most I/O deals with files, protection is 
extended to file systems:
–Read/Write/Execute permissions.
•Owner, group, and public access control

Protection Domain
•Protection domain defines the set of 
resources (objects) and access rights available 
to a process.
•It provides a boundary of authority for 
processes, ensuring they access only the 
devices/files they are permitted to.
•Especially important for I/O systems since 
devices (disk, printer, network, etc.) are 
shared among users and must be protected.

Concept of Protection Domain
Domain = (Objects + Rights)
•Each process executes within a domain of protection, 
which specifies:
–Objects: Files, I/O devices, memory segments.
–Rights: Operations allowed on those objects (read, write, 
execute, control).
Examples
•Printer domain → Process has write rights (send data 
to print), but no read rights.
•Disk domain → Process has read/write access to files 
but no right to issue a format command.
•Terminal domain → Process may read input and write 
output, but not control device settings

Types of Domains
•User Domain
–Limited privileges.
–Example: User can print a file but cannot directly 
control the printer hardware.
•Kernel Domain
–Full privileges.
–Can execute all I/O instructions and control 
devices directly.

Domain Switching
•A process may need to switch domains to 
perform certain operations (e.g., a user 
process making a system call to access a disk).
•Switching ensures:
–Protection from unauthorized access.
–Controlled access to I/O devices only through the 
OS.

Protection Matrix Representation
•Protection can be modeled using a matrix:
–Rows → Domains (processes/users).
–Columns → Objects (files, devices).
–Entries → Rights.
Importance in I/O System
•Prevents one process from misusing or damaging 
devices.
•Ensures safe sharing of devices among multiple 
users.
•Isolates faulty processes so that they do not 
corrupt global I/O resources.

Access Control List (ACL) 
•ACL is a protection mechanism used to specify 
which users or processes can access a 
resource (object) and what operations they 
are allowed to perform.
•Widely applied to I/O systems (disks, printers, 
terminals, files, etc.) to prevent unauthorized 
access.
•ACL is associated with each object (device, 
file, directory) that specifies.

Structure of ACL
Each entry in an ACL contains:
–User/Process ID
–Rights/Permissions (e.g., Read, Write, 
Execute, Control)

ACL in I/O System
•ACLs control which processes can interact with 
I/O devices and how.
Examples:
–Disk → Admin has Read/Write, users have Read only.
–Printer → Faculty and students can Write (print jobs), 
only admin can Control (cancel jobs, configure 
printer).
–Network device → Admin can Control, normal users 
can Send/Receive.
•

Advantages of ACL
•Fine-grained control → Different rights for 
different users.
•Flexibility → Supports multiple types of access 
(read, write, execute, control).
•Security → Prevents unauthorized or 
malicious use of I/O devices.
•Transparency → Easy to check permissions for 
each object.

Disadvantages of ACL
•Complexity → Large systems may have long 
ACLs (hard to manage).
•Performance Overhead → Checking ACLs may 
slow down access.
•Revocation Difficulty → Removing rights for 
many users can be tedious.

Development of Unix/Linux 
•Unix and Linux are foundational operating 
systems in computing history.
•They influenced many modern OSes like BSD, 
macOS, Android, and even Windows 
subsystems.
•Known for multi-user, multi-tasking, 
portability, and open-source philosophy.

Origins of Unix
•1969 – Developed at AT&T Bell Labs by Ken 
Thompson, Dennis Ritchie, and others.
•Written originally in assembly language for PDP-
7.
•Later rewritten in C language (1973) → made it 
portable across hardware platforms.
•Introduced key concepts:
–Hierarchical file system
–Pipes and filters
–Shell as a command interpreter
–Process control and multitasking

Evolution of Unix
•1970s – BSD Unix (University of California, 
Berkeley):
–Added C shell (csh), networking (TCP/IP), 
virtual memory.
•1980s – Commercial Unix:
–AT&T System V, SunOS, HP-UX, AIX.
•1990s – Fragmentation:
–Many versions existed, leading to the POSIX 
standard for compatibility.

Linux Development Timeline
•1991 – Linux 0.01 released (basic kernel, limited 
functionality).
•1992 – Linux licensed under GPL → community 
contributions.
•1994 – Linux 1.0 (first stable release).
•2000s – Popular for servers, supercomputers.
•2008 onwards – Android (Linux-based) 
dominates mobile OS market.
•Today – Linux powers >90% of supercomputers, 
most servers, IoT devices, and cloud systems.

Features of Unix/Linux
•Unix:
–Stable, multi-user, security-focused.
–Proprietary (in most forms).
•Linux:
–Open-source, free, community-driven.
–Highly customizable (distros like Ubuntu, Fedora, 
Red Hat).
–Portable across hardware (PCs, servers, embedded 
devices).

Unix vs Linux (Comparison Table)
FeatureUnixLinux
OriginAT&T Bell Labs, 1969Linus Torvalds, 1991
LicensingMostly proprietaryOpen-source (GPL)
PortabilityLimited, vendor-specificHigh portability, wide hardware
CostExpensive (commercial)Free & open-source
UsageServers, mainframesServers, desktops, mobiles, IoT

Role of Kernel in Unix/Linux
•The Kernel is the core component of 
Unix/Linux operating systems.
•Acts as a bridge between hardware and user-
level applications.
•Runs in privileged mode (kernel mode) with 
full control over system resources.

Types of Kernel Architectures
•Monolithic Kernel (Unix, traditional Linux):
–All core services (process, memory, file system, device 
drivers) run in kernel space.
–High performance, but large code base.
•Microkernel (MINIX, Mach):
–Minimal kernel functions (only essential services).
–Other services run in user space.
–More modular, but slower due to context switching.
•Hybrid Kernel:
–Mix of both (e.g., modern macOS, Windows NT).

Introduction to Linux Commands
•Linux is a command-line-based operating 
system where users interact with the system 
using commands.
•Each command has a specific function such as 
creating files, managing directories, 
monitoring processes, or configuring the 
system.
•Commands are typed into a shell/terminal 
and usually follow this format:

File and Directory Management Command
•pwd (Print Working Directory)
–Displays the current directory path.
–Example: pwd
•ls (List files and directories)
–Lists files and directories in the current folder.
–Options:
•ls -l → long format
•ls -a → includes hidden files
•cd (Change Directory)
–Changes the working directory.
–Example:
•cd Documents → go to Documents
•cd .. → move one step back

Cont...
•mkdir (Make Directory)
–Creates a new directory.
–Example: mkdir myfolder
•rmdir (Remove Directory)
–Removes an empty directory.
–Example: rmdir myfolder
•touch (Create File)
–Creates an empty file.
–Example: touch file.txt
•cat (Concatenate / View File)
–Displays file contents.
–Example: cat file.txt

Cont...
•cp (Copy File/Directory)
–Copies files or directories.
–Example: cp file.txt copy.txt
•mv (Move/Rename)
–Moves or renames a file.
–Example: mv file.txt newfile.txt
•rm (Remove File)
–Deletes a file.
–Example: rm file.txt

System Information Commands
•whoami – Displays the current logged-in user.
•uname – Displays system information (uname 
-a for detailed info).
•date – Displays current date and time.
•cal – Shows calendar of the current month.
•clear – Clears the terminal screen.

File Viewing and Editing
•nano filename – Opens file in Nano text editor.
•vi filename – Opens file in Vi editor.
•less filename – Views large files page by page.
User and Permission Management
•who – Shows logged-in users.
•id – Displays user ID (UID) and group ID (GID).
•chmod – Changes file permissions. Example: 
chmod 755 file.sh
•chown – Changes ownership of a file. Example: 
chown user:group file.txt

Shell Programming 
•Shell is a command interpreter in Linux/Unix 
that allows users to interact with the 
operating system.
•It accepts commands from the user and sends 
them to the kernel for execution.
•Examples of shells:
–Bash (Bourne Again Shell) – most common
–sh (Bourne Shell)
–csh (C Shell)
–ksh (Korn Shell)

What is Shell Programming?
•Writing a series of commands in a file (called 
a shell script) to automate tasks.
•Shell scripts can:
–Execute multiple commands sequentially.
–Handle decision-making (if/else).
–Perform looping operations(while, do-while).
–Take input and display output.

Input/Output (I/O) Software
•I/O System: Manages all input and output operations 
between the computer and external devices (disk, 
printer, keyboard, display, etc.).
•I/O Software: A set of system software components 
that provide a framework for I/O operations.
•Goals:
–Provide abstraction (hide hardware details).
–Ensure efficiency (fast data transfer).
–Guarantee protection (controlled access).

Goals of I/O Software
•Device Independence → Applications should work 
with any device without modification.
•Uniform Naming → All devices accessed in a 
standard way (e.g., Linux treats devices as files).
•Error Handling → Consistent error reporting across 
devices.
•Buffering → Handle speed mismatch between CPU 
and devices.
•Concurrency → Support multiple processes 
accessing I/O simultaneously.
•Portability → Applications portable across systems.

Device-independent I/O Software
• I/O Software: Provides mechanisms for 
communication between CPU, memory, and 
peripheral devices.
•Device-independent I/O software abstracts 
hardware differences and offers a uniform 
interface for applications.
•Ensures portability, ease of programming, 
and system efficiency.

Goals of Device-Independent I/O Software
•Uniformity: Same operations for all devices (e.g., 
read, write).
•Portability: Applications do not depend on 
specific device hardware.
•Error Handling: Provides consistent error 
detection and reporting.
•Buffering: Smooths out speed differences 
between devices and CPU.
•Naming: Provides a consistent naming scheme 
(file names, device paths).
•Protection: Ensures controlled access to devices.

Functions of Device-Independent I/O Software
•Uniform Naming of Devices
–Devices are identified using symbolic names (e.g., 
/dev/disk1).
–Hides hardware-specific details like device numbers.
•Device Protection
–Restricts access to devices using permissions (read, 
write, execute).
–Prevents multiple processes from conflicting over 
device use.
•Blocking/Non-blocking Operations
–Blocking: Process waits until I/O completes.
–Non-blocking: Process continues execution, I/O 
happens in the background.

Goals of Device-Independent I/O Software
1. Buffering
–Temporary storage of data during transfer.
–Types:
•Single Buffering: One buffer between device 
and process.
•Double Buffering: Two buffers for overlapping 
computation and I/O.
•Circular Buffering: Multiple buffers arranged in 
a ring.

Goals of Device-Independent I/O Software
2. Error Handling
–Reports errors in a consistent way (timeouts, 
device failures).
–Allows retry or recovery without exposing 
device-specific details.
3. Device-Independent Access
–Provides system calls like open(), read(), 
write(), close() applicable to files, disks, or 
terminals.



Layers of I/O Software
(a) User-Level I/O Software
•Part of application programs.
•Uses standard libraries (e.g., printf, scanf, fopen, 
fread).
•Simplifies programmer’s work.
(b) Device-Independent I/O Software
–Provides uniform interface to all devices.
–Functions: Naming, protection, buffering, 
error reporting.

(c) Device Drivers
–Device-specific code in the OS kernel.
–Converts generic OS requests into device 
commands.
–Example: Printer driver, graphics driver.
(d) Interrupt Handlers
–Part of OS that responds to hardware 
interrupts.
–Alerts CPU when I/O operation completes.
–(e) Hardware Layer
–Actual I/O devices and controllers.
–Examples: Disk, keyboard, display, network 
card.

I/O Techniques
•Programmed I/O
–CPU actively waits and checks device status.
–Simple but wastes CPU time.
•Interrupt-Driven I/O
–Device interrupts CPU when ready.
–More efficient than polling.
•Direct Memory Access (DMA)
–Special controller transfers data between device 
and memory without CPU intervention.
–CPU is free for other tasks.

Device Drivers
•Device Driver: A software module that acts as 
an intermediary between the operating 
system and a hardware device.
•Purpose: To provide the OS with a standard 
interface to communicate with hardware 
without needing to know device-specific 
details.
•Example: Printer driver, graphics card driver, 
keyboard driver.

Role of Device Drivers
•Hardware Abstraction
–Hides device-specific details from the OS.
–Provides a uniform API for applications and the kernel.
•Command Translation
–Converts OS/system calls (like read, write) into device-
specific commands.
•Communication Management
–Transfers data between I/O devices and main memory.
•Interrupt Handling
–Handles device-generated interrupts and notifies the OS 
when tasks are completed.
•Error Detection & Handling
–Detects device errors and reports them in a standardized 
format.

Functions of Device Drivers
•Initialization: Configure device settings at 
startup.
•Device Control: Send commands to the 
device.
•Data Transfer: Move data to/from device and 
memory.
•Status Monitoring: Report current device 
status to OS.
•Synchronization: Manage concurrent access 
requests.

Types of Device Drivers
•Character Device Drivers
–Handle character-based I/O (one byte/character at a 
time).
–Examples: Keyboard, mouse, serial ports.
•Block Device Drivers
–Handle block-based data transfer (fixed-size blocks).
–Examples: Hard disk, SSD, CD-ROM.
•Virtual Device Drivers
–Do not correspond to physical devices; instead, 
emulate a device in software.
–Example: Virtual printers, virtual network adapters.

Device Driver Architecture in OS
•Application Programs → request I/O via 
system calls (open, read, write).
•OS I/O Subsystem → passes request to 
appropriate driver.
•Device Driver → translates request to device-
specific commands.
•Hardware Device → performs the actual 
operation and sends status back.

Interrupt Handlers
•When an I/O device needs attention (e.g., 
data is ready to be read), it sends an interrupt 
signal to the CPU, which triggers the 
appropriate interrupt handler to perform the 
necessary actions.
Hardware:
•This is the physical layer, including the actual 
devices and their controllers, which the device 
drivers interact with directly.

Security Environment 
•A Security Environment in OS defines the 
framework of policies, mechanisms, and tools that 
protect the system against threats.
•It provides a safe execution environment for 
processes, users, and applications by ensuring 
confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
1. Components of Security Environment
•The OS security environment consists of multiple 
layers:

❑User-Level Security
•Authentication: Verifies identity of users (passwords, 
biometrics, tokens).
•Authorization: Defines what an authenticated user can 
access (files, processes, devices).
•User Roles: Admin, normal users, and guest accounts 
with different privileges.
❑Process-Level Security
•Isolation: Processes are separated to prevent 
interference.
•Memory Protection: Prevents one process from 
accessing another process’s memory.
•Execution Modes: User Mode vs. Kernel Mode ensures 
limited access.

❑File and Data Security
•File Permissions: Read, Write, Execute (e.g., Unix 
rwx model).
•Encryption: Protects sensitive files and 
communications.
•Access Control Lists (ACLs): Define fine-grained 
access rights.
❑Network Security
•Firewalls: Prevent unauthorized external access.
•Intrusion Detection/Prevention: Detect and 
block malicious activities.
•Secure Protocols: HTTPS, SSH, VPNs for secure 
communication.

❑System Monitoring and Auditing
•Audit Logs: Record user and system activities.
•Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) and Antivirus.
•System Updates and Patches: Prevent exploitation of 
vulnerabilities

2. Security Environment Goals
•Confidentiality – Prevent unauthorized access 
to data.
•Integrity – Ensure data accuracy and prevent 
tampering.
•Availability – Guarantee system resources are 
accessible to authorized users.
•Accountability – Record user actions for 
auditing.
•Non-repudiation – Ensure users cannot deny 
their actions.

3. Threats to Security Environment
•Internal Threats: Privilege abuse, careless 
users.
•External Threats: Hackers, malware, phishing.
•System Threats: Worms, viruses, Trojan 
horses.
•Network Threats: Denial-of-Service (DoS), 
spoofing, packet sniffing

4. Mechanisms to Maintain Security Environment
•Authentication Mechanisms
–Passwords, biometrics, smart cards, multi-factor 
authentication.
•Access Control Models
–DAC (Discretionary Access Control)
–MAC (Mandatory Access Control)
–RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)
•Cryptography
–Data encryption (AES, RSA) and digital signatures.

Cont...
•System Hardening
–Disabling unused ports/services, applying patches, 
enforcing security policies.
•Monitoring & Auditing
–Logging, intrusion detection, and auditing for 
suspicious activities.

Security Design Principles 
Security Design Principles 
•The design of an OS to make it secure, 
reliable, and resistant to attacks.
•Its ensure that system resources are protected 
against unauthorized access, misuse, and 
failures.
•These principles were first formalized by 
Saltzer and Schroeder

Principle of Least Privilege
•Each process, program, or user should have 
only the minimum access rights needed to 
perform its task.
•Reduces damage in case of compromise.
Example: Normal users cannot install system 
software; only administrators can.

Principle of Fail-Safe Defaults
•Default state should deny access, and explicit 
permission must be given to allow access.
•Prevents accidental access to resources.
Example: File permissions in Linux default to 
restricted unless granted

Principle of Economy of Mechanism
•Security design should be kept simple and small.
•Easier to analyze, verify, and implement.
Example: Unix “rwx” permission model is simple 
and effective.
Principle of Complete Mediation
•Every access request should be checked for 
authorization.
•No caching or bypassing of security checks.
Example: Each file access in modern OS goes 
through the kernel’s access control checks

Principle of Open Design
•Security should not depend on secrecy of design or 
implementation, but on secrecy of keys/passwords.
•Promotes trust and allows peer review.
Example: OpenSSL is publicly available but secure 
because of cryptographic keys.
Principle of Separation of Privilege
•Access to critical resources should require multiple 
conditions or approvals.
•Reduces single point of failure.
Example: Two-factor authentication (password + OTP).

Principle of Least Common Mechanism
•Minimize sharing of resources among multiple 
users/processes.
•Prevents unintended information leakage.
Example: Separate user environments in multi-user 
OS.
Principle of Psychological Acceptability
•Security mechanisms should be easy to use and 
understand.
•If too complex, users may bypass or disable them.
Example: GUI-based security settings in Windows vs 
complex command-line settings

User Authentication
Authentication is the process of verifying the 
identity of a user before granting access to 
system resources.
•It ensures that only legitimate users can 
access files, applications, and system services.
•Plays a key role in OS security environment 
along with authorization and access control.

Goals of User Authentication
•Verify Identity – Confirm that the user is who 
they claim to be.
•Prevent Unauthorized Access – Block 
attackers or unauthorized users.
•Accountability – Record actions of 
authenticated users.
•Enable Authorization – Authentication is the 
first step before assigning permissions.

Methods of User Authentication
1. Password-Based Authentication
•Most common and simplest method.
•Weakness: Password guessing, brute force.
•Countermeasures: Strong pwds, hashing, pwd 
policies.
2. Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
•Uses two or more authentication factors for 
stronger security.
•Example: ATM access requires CARD + PIN.

Methods of User Authentication
3. Token-Based Authentication
•Uses hardware/software tokens that generate 
temporary codes (OTP).
•Example: RSA tokens, Google Authenticator.
4. Biometric Authentication
•Uses unique physical/behavioral traits.
•Examples: Fingerprint scanners, face unlock, retina 
scans.
•Weakness: Privacy issues, expensive, false 
acceptance/rejection rates.

5. Certificate-Based Authentication
•Uses digital certificates and public key 
cryptography.
•Common in secure networks, corporate systems.
6. Single Sign-On (SSO)
•User logs in once and gains access to multiple 
applications without re-authentication.
•Example: Google Account login across Gmail, 
Drive, YouTube.

Protection Mechanisms 
•In OS, protection mechanisms ensure that I/O 
devices and data are accessed securely and 
correctly.
•Protection is required to prevent unauthorized 
access, avoid data corruption, and provide 
controlled sharing among processes.
•Since I/O devices are shared resources, improper 
or malicious use can crash the system or 
compromise security.

Objectives I/O Protection
•Prevent unauthorized access to devices.
•Protect data integrity during read/write 
operations.
•Ensure controlled sharing among processes.
•Provide fault isolation so that one faulty 
process does not affect others.
•Enable safe execution of user programs by 
restricting direct access to hardware

Protection Mechanisms
(a) Dual Mode Operation
•CPU operates in two modes:
–User Mode: User programs run with limited 
privileges.
–Kernel (Supervisor) Mode: OS runs with full 
privileges.

(b) Access Control
•Controls which users/processes can access 
which devices.
•Achieved using:
–Access Control Lists (ACLs) → define permissions 
for each device.
–Capability Lists → specify what rights a process 
has.
•Prevents accidental or malicious misuse of 
devices.

(c) Memory Protection in I/O
•Ensures that I/O devices cannot overwrite 
memory of other processes.
•Achieved using:
–Base and Limit Registers → restricts memory 
access range.
–I/O Mapping → isolates I/O address space from 
main memory.

(d) I/O Privileged Instructions
•Certain I/O-related instructions (e.g., IN, OUT) 
are privileged.
•Executed only in kernel mode to avoid misuse.
•Example: A user program cannot directly send 
commands to disk controller

(e) Device Access Control
•OS uses Device Drivers to control access.
•Drivers implement:
–Queuing (to schedule multiple requests).
–Buffering (to prevent data loss).
–Error handling (to avoid system crashes).

(f) Interrupt Handling Protection
•Interrupts are used for device communication.
•OS ensures:
–Only authorized interrupt handlers run in kernel 
mode.
•Malicious code cannot replace handlers
(g) File System Protection
•Since most I/O deals with files, protection is 
extended to file systems:
–Read/Write/Execute permissions.
•Owner, group, and public access control

Protection Domain
•Protection domain defines the set of 
resources (objects) and access rights available 
to a process.
•It provides a boundary of authority for 
processes, ensuring they access only the 
devices/files they are permitted to.
•Especially important for I/O systems since 
devices (disk, printer, network, etc.) are 
shared among users and must be protected.

Concept of Protection Domain
Domain = (Objects + Rights)
•Each process executes within a domain of protection, 
which specifies:
–Objects: Files, I/O devices, memory segments.
–Rights: Operations allowed on those objects (read, write, 
execute, control).
Examples
•Printer domain → Process has write rights (send data 
to print), but no read rights.
•Disk domain → Process has read/write access to files 
but no right to issue a format command.
•Terminal domain → Process may read input and write 
output, but not control device settings

Types of Domains
•User Domain
–Limited privileges.
–Example: User can print a file but cannot directly 
control the printer hardware.
•Kernel Domain
–Full privileges.
–Can execute all I/O instructions and control 
devices directly.

Domain Switching
•A process may need to switch domains to 
perform certain operations (e.g., a user 
process making a system call to access a disk).
•Switching ensures:
–Protection from unauthorized access.
–Controlled access to I/O devices only through the 
OS.

Protection Matrix Representation
•Protection can be modeled using a matrix:
–Rows → Domains (processes/users).
–Columns → Objects (files, devices).
–Entries → Rights.
Importance in I/O System
•Prevents one process from misusing or damaging 
devices.
•Ensures safe sharing of devices among multiple 
users.
•Isolates faulty processes so that they do not 
corrupt global I/O resources.

Access Control List (ACL) 
•ACL is a protection mechanism used to specify 
which users or processes can access a 
resource (object) and what operations they 
are allowed to perform.
•Widely applied to I/O systems (disks, printers, 
terminals, files, etc.) to prevent unauthorized 
access.
•ACL is associated with each object (device, 
file, directory) that specifies.

Structure of ACL
Each entry in an ACL contains:
–User/Process ID
–Rights/Permissions (e.g., Read, Write, 
Execute, Control)

ACL in I/O System
•ACLs control which processes can interact with 
I/O devices and how.
Examples:
–Disk → Admin has Read/Write, users have Read only.
–Printer → Faculty and students can Write (print jobs), 
only admin can Control (cancel jobs, configure 
printer).
–Network device → Admin can Control, normal users 
can Send/Receive.
•

Advantages of ACL
•Fine-grained control → Different rights for 
different users.
•Flexibility → Supports multiple types of access 
(read, write, execute, control).
•Security → Prevents unauthorized or 
malicious use of I/O devices.
•Transparency → Easy to check permissions for 
each object.

Disadvantages of ACL
•Complexity → Large systems may have long 
ACLs (hard to manage).
•Performance Overhead → Checking ACLs may 
slow down access.
•Revocation Difficulty → Removing rights for 
many users can be tedious.

Development of Unix/Linux 
•Unix and Linux are foundational operating 
systems in computing history.
•They influenced many modern OSes like BSD, 
macOS, Android, and even Windows 
subsystems.
•Known for multi-user, multi-tasking, 
portability, and open-source philosophy.

Origins of Unix
•1969 – Developed at AT&T Bell Labs by Ken 
Thompson, Dennis Ritchie, and others.
•Written originally in assembly language for PDP-
7.
•Later rewritten in C language (1973) → made it 
portable across hardware platforms.
•Introduced key concepts:
–Hierarchical file system
–Pipes and filters
–Shell as a command interpreter
–Process control and multitasking

Evolution of Unix
•1970s – BSD Unix (University of California, 
Berkeley):
–Added C shell (csh), networking (TCP/IP), 
virtual memory.
•1980s – Commercial Unix:
–AT&T System V, SunOS, HP-UX, AIX.
•1990s – Fragmentation:
–Many versions existed, leading to the POSIX 
standard for compatibility.

Linux Development Timeline
•1991 – Linux 0.01 released (basic kernel, limited 
functionality).
•1992 – Linux licensed under GPL → community 
contributions.
•1994 – Linux 1.0 (first stable release).
•2000s – Popular for servers, supercomputers.
•2008 onwards – Android (Linux-based) 
dominates mobile OS market.
•Today – Linux powers >90% of supercomputers, 
most servers, IoT devices, and cloud systems.

Features of Unix/Linux
•Unix:
–Stable, multi-user, security-focused.
–Proprietary (in most forms).
•Linux:
–Open-source, free, community-driven.
–Highly customizable (distros like Ubuntu, Fedora, 
Red Hat).
–Portable across hardware (PCs, servers, embedded 
devices).

Unix vs Linux (Comparison Table)
FeatureUnixLinux
OriginAT&T Bell Labs, 1969Linus Torvalds, 1991
LicensingMostly proprietaryOpen-source (GPL)
PortabilityLimited, vendor-specificHigh portability, wide hardware
CostExpensive (commercial)Free & open-source
UsageServers, mainframesServers, desktops, mobiles, IoT

Role of Kernel in Unix/Linux
•The Kernel is the core component of 
Unix/Linux operating systems.

full control over system resources.

Types of Kernel Architectures

–All core services (process, memory, file system, device 
drivers) run in kernel space.
–High performance, but large code base.
•Microkernel (MINIX, Mach):
