–Other services run in user space.
–More modular, but slower due to context switching.
•Hybrid Kernel:
–Mix of both (e.g., modern macOS, Windows NT).

Introduction to Linux Commands
•Linux is a command-line-based operating 
system where users interact with the system 
using commands.
•Each command has a specific function such as 
creating files, managing directories, 
monitoring processes, or configuring the 
system.
•Commands are typed into a shell/terminal 
and usually follow this format:

File and Directory Management Command
•pwd (Print Working Directory)
–Displays the current directory path.
–Example: pwd
•ls (List files and directories)
–Lists files and directories in the current folder.
–Options:
•ls -l → long format
•ls -a → includes hidden files
•cd (Change Directory)
–Changes the working directory.
–Example:
•cd Documents → go to Documents
•cd .. → move one step back

Cont...
•mkdir (Make Directory)
–Creates a new directory.
–Example: mkdir myfolder
•rmdir (Remove Directory)
–Removes an empty directory.
–Example: rmdir myfolder
•touch (Create File)
–Creates an empty file.
–Example: touch file.txt
•cat (Concatenate / View File)
–Displays file contents.
–Example: cat file.txt

Cont...
•cp (Copy File/Directory)
–Copies files or directories.
–Example: cp file.txt copy.txt
•mv (Move/Rename)
–Moves or renames a file.
–Example: mv file.txt newfile.txt
•rm (Remove File)
–Deletes a file.
–Example: rm file.txt

System Information Commands
•whoami – Displays the current logged-in user.
•uname – Displays system information (uname 
-a for detailed info).
•date – Displays current date and time.
•cal – Shows calendar of the current month.
•clear – Clears the terminal screen.

File Viewing and Editing
•nano filename – Opens file in Nano text editor.
•vi filename – Opens file in Vi editor.
•less filename – Views large files page by page.
User and Permission Management
•who – Shows logged-in users.
•id – Displays user ID (UID) and group ID (GID).
•chmod – Changes file permissions. Example: 
chmod 755 file.sh
•chown – Changes ownership of a file. Example: 
chown user:group file.txt

Shell Programming 
•Shell is a command interpreter in Linux/Unix 
that allows users to interact with the 
operating system.
•It accepts commands from the user and sends 
them to the kernel for execution.
•Examples of shells:
–Bash (Bourne Again Shell) – most common
–sh (Bourne Shell)
–csh (C Shell)
–ksh (Korn Shell)

What is Shell Programming?
•Writing a series of commands in a file (called 
a shell script) to automate tasks.
•Shell scripts can:
–Execute multiple commands sequentially.
–Handle decision-making (if/else).
–Perform looping operations(while, do-while).
–Take input and display output.

textContent:
UNIT-5
 Operating Systems & System Programming
(23ACA06)
 

Programming Language Grammars and Classification
•In compiler design, understanding programming 
language grammars is critical for scanning 
(lexical analysis) and parsing (syntax analysis).
•Scanning: Converts raw source code into tokens.
•Parsing: Checks token sequences against the 
grammar rules to ensure syntactic correctness.
•Grammars define the syntax rules of a language 
and help in constructing lexical analyzers and 
parsers.
2

Lexical Analysis (Scanning):
–Uses regular grammar to identify tokens like 
identifiers, keywords, numbers, operators.
–Token examples: id, number, if, while, +, -
–Finite automata are used to recognize regular 
patterns.
Syntax Analysis (Parsing):
–Uses context-free grammar (CFG) to check valid 
arrangement of tokens.
–Builds parse trees to represent program structure.
–Detects syntax errors and ensures compliance 
with language rules.
3

Components of Programming Language Grammar
A formal grammar 퐺is defined as:
푮=푵푻푷푺
4
ComponentDescriptionExample
NNon-terminal symbols
<expression>, 
<statement>
TTerminal symbolsid, +, *, if, ;
PProduction rules
<expression> → 
<expression> + 
<term>
SStart symbol<program>

Classification of Grammars in Scanning and Parsing
TypeGrammarUsed In
Type 3 (Regular 
Grammar)
Can be represented 
by regular expressions
Scanning / Lexical 
Analysis
Type 2 (Context-Free 
Grammar - CFG)
Non-terminals → 
terminals & non-
terminals
Parsing / Syntax 
Analysis
Type 1 (Context-
Sensitive Grammar)
Non-terminals → 
strings depending on 
context
Rarely used in 
practical compilers
Type 0 (Unrestricted 
Grammar)
No restrictions
Theoretical 
computation models
5

Scanning (Lexical Analysis)
Objective: Convert source code into tokens.
Input: Source code string
Process: Matches sequences of characters with 
regular expressions (patterns for identifiers, 
literals, operators)
Output: Stream of tokens for the parser
6

Example:
  Source code: x = a + 5;
LexemeToken
xid
=assignment
aid
+plus
5number
;semicolon
7

Parsing (Syntax Analysis)
•Objective: Ensure token sequences conform 
to grammar rules (CFG).
•Input: Token stream from scanner
•Process: Builds parse tree using parsing 
techniques
–Top-Down Parsing: Starts from start symbol and 
predicts tokens
–Bottom-Up Parsing: Builds tree from tokens 
towards start symbol
•Output: Parse tree or syntax error messages
8

Connection Between Grammar Type and Compiler 
Phases
PhaseGrammar TypePurpose

Grammar

structure

Analysis

Sensitive Rules

scope 
resolution
9

Ambiguity in Grammatic Specification
•A grammar 퐺 is ambiguous if there exists at 
least one string ′풘’ in the language 퐿퐺that 
has two or more distinct parse trees (or 
derivations).
Formally:
•∃풘∈푳푮:풘has ≥ퟐ parse trees according
 to 퐺
10

Example of Ambiguous Grammar
•Consider the arithmetic expression grammar: E → E + E 
| E * E | (E) | id
•String: id + id * id
•Parse Tree 1: + evaluated before * 
        E
      /|\      
     E + E
     |   |
    id   E
        / \
       E   E
       |   |
      id  id
11

•Parse Tree 2: * evaluated before +
       E
      /|\
     E * E
     |   |
    E    id
   / \
  id  id
Problem: Same expression can be interpreted 
differently → ambiguity.
12

Causes of Ambiguity
•Operator Precedence Not Defined
–Example: + and * in arithmetic expressions.
•Multiple Parsing Rules
–More than one production can generate the same 
string.
•Optional Symbols / Recursion
–Left recursion and optional rules may lead to 
multiple derivations.
13

Problems Caused by Ambiguity
•Parser Confusion: The parser may not know 
which tree to build.
•Multiple Interpretations: Programs may have 
different meanings, causing errors in 
execution.
•Complicates Compiler Design: Ambiguity 
needs resolution to generate consistent code.
14

Detecting Ambiguity
•Construct parse trees for the same string.
•If more than one valid parse tree exists → 
grammar is ambiguous.
•Some grammars are intrinsically ambiguous 
(no equivalent unambiguous grammar exists).
15

Resolving Ambiguity
1. Redefining Grammar
Modify production rules to remove multiple derivations.
Example: Resolve arithmetic expression ambiguity:
E → E + T | T
T → T * F | F
F → (E) | id
* has higher precedence than +
This grammar is unambiguous for expressions like id + id 
* id.
16

Resolving Ambiguity
2. Use Operator Precedence and Associativity
Specify:
Precedence: Which operator is applied first
Associativity: Left-to-right or right-to-left evaluation
3. Parser Directives
Some parser generators (e.g., Yacc/Bison) allow 
disambiguation rules:
%left, %right, %nonassoc for operators
17

Summary Table
AspectDescription
Ambiguity
A grammar is ambiguous if a string has 
multiple parse trees
Cause
Undefined operator precedence, multiple 
production rules
Problem
Parser confusion, multiple 
interpretations, compiler errors
Solution
Redefine grammar, specify 
precedence/associativity, use parser 
directives
18

Scanning
•Scanning(Lexical Analysis.) is the first phase of 
compilation in system programming. 
•Purpose: scanning is to read the source program 
(sequence of characters) and convert into sequence 
of tokens.
•Scanning (Lexical Analysis) is the process of reading 
the source code character by character and 
grouping them into meaningful sequences called 
lexemes, which are then converted into tokens.
19

Scanning Diagram
20

Role of the Scanner
•Acts as a bridge between the source code and 
the parser.
•Simplifies the parser’s job by identifying valid 
words or symbols (tokens) from raw input 
text.
•Example: int a = b + 10;
21

Important Terms
TermDescriptionExample
Lexeme
Actual character sequence 
from source code
int, a, 10
Token
Symbolic name for a 
lexeme
KEYWORD, 
IDENTIFIER, 
NUMBER
Pattern
Rule that defines how to 
form lexemes
e.g., Identifier → 
[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-
9]*
22

Functions of the Scanner
•Read input characters
–Reads the program one character at a time.
•Remove whitespace and comments
–Skips irrelevant data to make parsing easier.
•Recognize tokens
–Groups characters into keywords, identifiers, 
numbers, symbols, etc.
•Report lexical errors
–Detects invalid characters or illegal identifiers.
•Communicate with the symbol table
–Stores identifiers and related information for later 
stages.
23

Token Types
Token TypeExample
Keywordsif, else, int, while
Identifierssum, count, total
Constants10, 3.14, 'A'
Operators+, -, *, /, =
Punctuators / 
Delimiters
;, ,, (, )
24

Parsing
•Parsing is the second phase of compilation in System 
Programming.
•The parser receives a stream of tokens from the 
scanner and checks whether the sequence of tokens 
follows the syntax rules of the programming 
language.
•Parsing (Syntax Analysis) is the process of analyzing 
a sequence of tokens to determine its grammatical 
structure according to the rules of a context-free 
grammar (CFG).
25

Parsing Diagram
26

Role of Parser
•Takes tokens from the scanner.
•Applies grammar rules to construct a parse 
tree.
•Detects syntax errors in the source program 
and Provides structural information to the 
next compiler phase (semantic analysis).
Example source code:
  a = b + c * d;
27

Parse Tree
•A Parse Tree is a hierarchical tree structure 
that shows how a string (or program) is 
derived from the start symbol of a grammar 
using the production rules of that grammar.
•A Parse Tree is a graphical representation of 
how a parser analyzes and derives a source 
code’s structure according to grammar rules.
28

Key Points of Parse Tree
•Each internal node in the tree represents a 
non-terminal symbol.
•Each leaf node represents a terminal symbol 
(tokens such as identifiers, operators, or 
keywords).
•The root node represents the start symbol of 
the grammar.
•The tree shows the derivation steps of the 
input string.
29

Grammar in Parsing
A grammar defines the syntactic structure of a 
programming language using production rules.
G = (V, T, P, S)
Where:
•V → set of non-terminal symbols
•T → set of terminal symbols (tokens)
•P → set of production rules
•S → start symbol
30

Types of Parsing
TypeDescriptionExample
Top-Down 
Parsing
Begins from the start 
symbol and tries to derive 
the input string
Recursive 
Descent, LL(1)
Bottom-Up 
Parsing
Begins with input and tries 
to reduce it to the start 
symbol
Shift-Reduce, 
LR(0), SLR(1)
31

1. Top-Down Parsing
•Tries to predict which production rule to use.
•Works from root to leaves of the parse tree.
•Example methods:
–Recursive Descent Parser
–Predictive (LL(1)) Parser
•Example:
For grammar E → E + T | T,
parser starts with E and tries to derive the 
input id + id.
32

Bottom-Up Parsing
•Works from leaves (input tokens) to root 
(start symbol).
•Repeatedly reduces input string using 
grammar rules.
Example methods:
–Shift-Reduce Parser
–LR Parser (LR(0), SLR(1), LALR(1))
33

Difference Between Scanning and Parsing
AspectScanningParsing
PhaseFirst phaseSecond phase
Input
Source program 
(characters)
Tokens
OutputTokensParse tree
ChecksLexical structureSyntax structure
Error TypeLexical errorsSyntax errors
34

Compilers and Interpreters
•A Compiler is a system software that translates a 
high-level source program (like C, Java) into machine 
code (object code) that the computer can execute 
directly.
•The entire program is translated before execution.
•Errors are reported after the compilation process is 
complete.
Example:
  C compiler, C++ compiler, Java compiler (javac)
35

Interpreters
•An Interpreter is a system program that translates 
and executes a source program line-by-line or 
statement-by-statement.
•Each instruction is translated and executed 
immediately.
•Errors are reported one at a time, stopping execution 
when found.
Example:
  Python interpreter, JavaScript engine, PHP 
interpreter
36

Compilation Process
•The compilation process converts a high-level 
language program into machine-level code 
through several phases. Each phase performs 
a specific task in translating and optimizing 
the program.
37

Phases of Compiler
38

Phases of Compiler
1. Lexical Analysis (Scanning)
Breaks the source code into tokens (keywords, 
identifiers, literals, operators).
Tool used: Lexical Analyzer (Lexer).
Output: Token stream.
2. Syntax Analysis (Parsing)
Checks if the sequence of tokens follows the grammar 
rules of the language.
Builds a Parse Tree or Syntax Tree.
Tool used: Parser.
39

Phases of Compilation:
3. Semantic Analysis
Ensures the meaning of the program is correct.
Checks type compatibility, variable declarations, and 
scope rules.
Output: Annotated Syntax Tree.
4. Intermediate Code Generation
Produces a machine-independent intermediate code.
Easier for optimization and translation.
Example format: Three-address code.
40

Phases of Compilation:
5. Code Optimization
Improves efficiency of intermediate code without 
changing its meaning.
Reduces execution time and memory usage.
6. Code Generation
Converts optimized intermediate code into machine 
code (target code).
Output: Object code.
7. Code Linking and Loading
Links different object files and loads the final 
executable into memory.
41

Semantic Gap
•The Semantic Gap refers to the difference between 
the high-level human understanding of a problem 
and the low-level operations that the computer 
hardware performs.
•It measures how far apart the source language (user-
oriented) and machine language (hardware-
oriented) are in abstraction level.
42

Example:
Writing sum = a + b in C is simple for a human.
The computer must perform:
–LOAD a → Register1
–LOAD b → Register2
–ADD Register1, Register2 → Register3
–STORE Register3 → sum(C)
•This gap in understanding and execution is the semantic gap.
43

Binding and Scope Rules
•Binding is the process of associating an attribute 
with an entity such as a variable, data type, or 
function in a program.
•Binding of a variable to its data type:
   int x;
→ binds variable x to type int
•Binding of a variable to its memory location (during 
execution)
•Binding of a function name to its definition
44

Types of Binding
TypeDescriptionExample
Static Binding 
(Early Binding)
Binding that occurs before 
program execution, usually 
during compilation
Data type of a 
variable, function 
overloading 
resolution
Dynamic 
Binding (Late 
Binding)
Binding that occurs during 
execution (runtime)
Function overriding in 
object-oriented 
languages like Java or 
Python
45

Phases of Binding in Compilation
Binding TypeTime of Occurrence
Language 
Definition Time
Keywords, syntax, semantics defined
Compile TimeVariable types, procedure calls
Link TimeExternal references resolved
Load TimeAddresses assigned
Run TimeDynamic bindings (values, objects) assigned
46

Scope Rules
•Scope of a variable is the region of a program 
where that variable is visible and can be used.
•Scope determines which variable name refers to 
which binding in nested or overlapping context
Types of Scope
•1. Static Scope (Lexical Scope)
•2. Dynamic Scope
47

1. Static Scope (Lexical Scope)
–Determined at compile time based on program 
structure.
–The compiler uses textual (lexical) structure of 
the code to resolve variable references.
–Most modern languages (C, Java, Python) use 
static scope.
Example: 
x = 10;
def func():
    x = 20;
    print(x)
func()     # Output: 20
print(x)  # Output: 20
48

2. Dynamic Scope
•Determined at runtime, based on the calling 
sequence of functions.
•A variable is searched in the current function and 
then in the calling functions.
•Used in older languages (like early Lisp versions).
Example: 
•Function A defines x = 5
•Function B prints x
•If B is called by A → B uses A’s x (dynamic 
lookup)
49

Memory Allocation
Memory allocation is the process of reserving 
portions of computer memory for storing program 
instructions and data during program execution.
•In simple terms, when a program runs, it needs 
memory for:
–Program code (instructions)
–Variables and data structures
–Function calls (stack)
–Dynamic data (heap)
•The compiler, linker, loader, and operating system 
work together to manage how memory is allocated.
50

Types of Memory Allocation
1.Static Memory Allocation: 
•In static allocation, memory for all variables is 
allocated during compile time.
•The size and location of each variable are fixed 
before program execution begins.
Example:
int a = 10;
float b = 5.5;
char name[20];
All variables above are assigned fixed memory locations 
at compile time.
51

Characteristics:
•Memory is allocated once and cannot change 
during execution.
•Allocation is handled by the compiler.
•Faster execution because addresses are 
known beforehand.
•Less flexible.
52

Advantages:
•Simple to implement.
•No runtime overhead.
•Faster access.
Disadvantages:
•Memory is wasted if not fully used.
•The program cannot handle variable-size data 
(e.g., dynamic arrays).
53

2. Dynamic Memory Allocation
•In dynamic allocation, memory is allocated at 
runtime based on program needs.
•This allows programs to request memory while 
executing.
Characteristics:
•Done during program execution.
•Managed using heap memory.
•Requires pointers to access dynamically allocated 
memory.
•Programmer must manually release memory 
when it’s no longer needed.
54

Example:
•int *ptr;
•ptr = (int *) malloc(sizeof(int) * 5); // Allocates 
memory for 5 integers.
Dynamic Allocation Functions in C:
55
FunctionPurpose
malloc()Allocates memory block
calloc()Allocates and initializes to zero
realloc()Reallocates or resizes memory
free()Frees allocated memory

Advantages:
•Efficient use of memory.
•Flexible — memory can grow or shrink as 
needed.
•Suitable for variable-size data structures (like 
linked lists, trees).
Disadvantages:
•Slightly slower due to runtime management.
•May cause memory leaks if not properly 
freed.
•Fragmentation may occur in the heap.
56

Memory Allocation Areas in a Program
SegmentPurpose
Code Segment 
(Text Segment)
Stores compiled instructions of the program
Data SegmentStores global and static variables
HeapUsed for dynamic memory allocation
StackUsed for function calls and local variables
Registers
Store temporary data and CPU-level 
operations
57

Storage Classes and Memory Allocation (in C)
Storage ClassScopeLifetimeMemory AreaExample
AUTOLocalFunction callStackauto int x;
REGISTERLocalFunction callCPU Registerregister int x;
STATICLocal/GlobalEntire programData Segmentstatic int x;
EXTERNGlobalEntire programData Segmentextern int x;
58

Code Optimization
•Code Optimization is the process of improving the 
intermediate or final code of a program so that it 
runs faster, uses less memory, or performs more 
efficiently — without changing its output or 
meaning.
•In phases of the compiler, typically performed after 
intermediate code generation and before final code 
generation.
59

Objective of Code Optimization
The main goals of code optimization are:
•Improve Execution Speed – Make the program run 
faster.
•Reduce Memory Usage – Use fewer variables and 
storage locations.
•Reduce Power Consumption – Important for 
embedded systems and mobile devices.
•Improve Resource Utilization – Optimize CPU and 
cache performance.
•Preserve Correctness – Ensure optimized code gives 
the same output as the original code.
60

Work of Code Optimization
61

Types of Code Optimization
1. Machine-Independent Optimization
2. Machine-Dependent Optimization
1. Machine-Independent Optimization
•Performed on intermediate code.
•Independent of target machine architecture.
•Focuses on reducing redundancy and improving 
logic.
62

Common Techniques:
TechniqueDescriptionExample
Constant Folding
Evaluate constant expressions at 
compile time.
x = 4 * 2 → x = 8
Constant 
Propagation
Replace known constant variables 
with their values.
a = 5; b = a + 3; → b = 8;
Dead Code 
Elimination
Remove code that never affects 
program output.
if (false) { ... } removed
Common 
Subexpression 
Elimination
Avoid recomputing the same 
expression multiple times.
x = a + b; y = a + b + c; → 
reuse a + b
Loop Invariant Code 
Motion
Move calculations outside loops if 
they don’t change within the loop.
for(i=0;i<n;i++) y=x*5; → 
move x*5 outside
Strength Reduction
Replace expensive operations with 
cheaper ones.
x = i * 2; → x = i + i;
Copy Propagation
Replace variables that only copy 
values from others.
a = b; c = a + 1; → c = b + 1;
Algebraic 
Simplification
Simplify expressions using algebraic 
rules.
x = x + 0 → x
63

2. Machine-Dependent Optimization
•Performed during target code generation.
•Depends on the architecture of the machine 
(e.g., register usage, instruction set).
•Improves execution speed by efficiently using 
CPU registers and machine instructions
64

Common Techniques:
TechniqueDescription
Register Allocation
Assign frequently used variables to CPU 
registers instead of memory.
Instruction 
Scheduling
Arrange instructions to minimize CPU idle 
time or pipeline stalls.
Loop OptimizationUnroll or optimize loops for speed.
Inline Expansion
Replace small function calls with the 
function body itself.
Peephole 
Optimization
Examine small sets of instructions 
(“peepholes”) and replace inefficient 
sequences with better ones.
65

Example:
Before Optimization:
MOV R1, A
MOV R2, A
After Optimization:
MOV R2, R1
66

Advantages of Code Optimization
AdvantageDescription
Faster ExecutionEliminates redundant instructions.
Reduced Memory UseRemoves unused variables and code.
Smaller Code SizeCompact and efficient code.
Improved PerformanceBetter use of CPU and cache.
Energy Efficiency
Important for mobile and embedded 
systems.
67

Compilation of Expressions and Control Structures
•During the compilation process, one of the main 
tasks of the code generation phase is to translate 
expressions and control structures (like loops 
and conditionals) into intermediate code or 
machine code that the computer can execute.
•This process ensures that high-level language 
constructs are converted into efficient low-level 
instructions.
68

1. Compilation of Expressions
•Expressions are the basic building blocks of any 
program.
They include arithmetic, relational, and logical 
operations.
Expression Types
•Arithmetic Expressions – 
  involve operators like +, -, *, /
•Relational Expressions – 
  involve operators like <, >, <=, ==
•Logical Expressions – 
  involve operators like &&, ||, !
69

Steps in Compilation:
•Parsing Phase:
–Converts expression into a parse tree:
•Intermediate Code Generation:
–The compiler converts the parse tree into three-
address code (TAC).
•Optimization (optional):
–Removes redundant computations or simplifies 
code.
•Code Generation:
–Translates intermediate code into assembly or 
machine code
70

2. Compilation of Control Structures
•Control structures define the order in which 
statements are executed.
The compiler must translate them into branching 
and looping instructions.
 If–Else Statement
•High-level code:                
if (a < b)
   x = 1;
else
   x = 2;
71

Flow of Control
StructureControl Flow TypeMechanism Used
SequentialStraight-line executionNone
SelectionConditional branchingif, else, switch
IterationRepeated executionfor, while, do-while
JumpUnconditionalgoto, break, continue
72

Overview of Interpreters and Debuggers
•Interpreter is a system program that executes 
the source program line by line translating  
HLL instructions into machine code at 
runtime.
Function:
•Reads a high-level language statement.
•Converts it to intermediate or machine code.
•Executes it immediately before moving to the 
next line.
73

Process of Interpreter
74

Key Features
•No separate object code is generated.
•Execution is slower than compiled programs.
•Useful for testing and debugging small 
programs.
Examples
•Python Interpreter (CPython)
•JavaScript Engine (V8)
•Ruby Interpreter
•MATLAB Interpreter
75

Advantages
•Easier debugging (error shown immediately).
•Ideal for scripting and interactive 
environments.
•Platform-independent execution.
76

Debugger
•A Debugger is a system program used to test 
and debug other programs by allowing the 
programmer to control execution and 
examine program state.
Function
•Helps identify logical, runtime, or syntax 
errors.
•Provides tools to pause, inspect, modify, and 
resume execution.
77

Process of Debugger
78

Key Features
•Breakpoints: Stop execution at specific lines.
•Step Execution: Execute code line by line.
•Watch Variables: Monitor variable values.
•Memory Dump: View memory and register 
contents.
•Call Stack Inspection: Trace function calls.
79

Examples
•GDB (GNU Debugger) – for C/C++
•PDB (Python Debugger)
•Visual Studio Debugger
•LLDB (LLVM Debugger)
Advantages
•Detects runtime and logic errors effectively.
•Allows tracing of program flow.
•Helps in optimizing code and performance.
80

C Development Tools
•C development on an Operating System (like 
Linux, macOS, or Windows) involves using 
command-line tools and shell utilities for 
editing, compiling, linking, executing, and 
debugging C programs.
•These tools form the software development 
environment that supports program creation 
and execution.
81

Common Commands / 
Tools

vi, vim, nano, gedit, 
notepad

code
as

into executable
ld

82

C Development Workflow Using Shell Commands
Step 1: Write the Source Code
Use a text editor to create a C source file.
$ vi hello.c
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    printf("Hello, World!\n");
    return 0;
}
83

Step 2: Compile the Source Code
–Use a compiler (like GCC) to compile and generate 
an executable file.
–$ gcc hello.c -o hello
–hello.c → Source file
–-o hello → Output executable name
Step 3: Execute the Program
–$ ./hello
Output:
–Hello, World!
84

85

Machine-Level Representation of Data and Programs
•at the machine level, 
 all data and programs are represented in 
binary form (0s and 1s).
•Computers can only understand and execute 
instructions expressed in machine language, 
which corresponds directly to the hardware 
architecture (processor design, instruction set, 
and memory organization).
86

Representation of Data
a) Binary Representation
The binary system uses two symbols: 0 and 1.
Each binary digit is called a bit.
A group of 8 bits forms a byte.
Example:
A → ASCII code → 01000001
87

b) Numeric Data Representation
TypeDescriptionExample
Integer
Represented using binary 
numbers (fixed number of 
bits).
00001010 = 10
Signed 
Integer
Uses Two’s Complement for 
negative numbers.
11110110 = –10
Floating Point
Represented using IEEE 754 
format (sign, exponent, 
mantissa).
01000001001000000
000000000000000 = 
10.0
BCD (Binary 
Coded 
Decimal)
Each decimal digit is 
represented by 4 bits.
59 → 0101 1001
88

c) Character Data Representation
Encoding 
Scheme
DescriptionExample
ASCII
7-bit code for English letters and 
symbols.
A → 65

Unicode
Supports global characters (16-bit or 
32-bit).
A → U+0041
89

d) Boolean Data
•Represented by single bits:
❑  0 → False
❑  1 → True
•Used in logical operations and control structures.
e) Pointer and Address Representation
•A pointer stores the memory address of a variable or 
instruction.
Example:
–Variable x stored at address 0x2000
–Pointer p = &x → value of p is 0x2000
90

3. Representation of Programs
a) Source Program
Written in a high-level language (like C, C++, Python).
Human-readable instructions.
Example:
 int sum = a + b;
b) Assembly Program
Translated from source code into assembly language 
(mnemonics).
Example:
MOV R1, a
ADD R1, b
MOV sum, R1
91

c) Machine Code
Binary form of the program executed by the CPU.
Example (for a simple ADD operation):
  10001011      00000001
Each instruction corresponds to an opcode 
(operation code) and operands.
92

d) Memory Representation of Programs
•When a program runs, it is divided into several 
segments:
93
SegmentPurpose
Code Segment (Text

File Input/Output
File I/O (Input/Output) refers to the process of:
Input → Reading data from files
Output → Writing data to files
In system-level programming, file I/O is 
managed through kernel-level system calls, 
such as:
open(), read(), write(), close(), lseek()
These calls interact directly with the file 
descriptors maintained by the operating system.

Concurrency in File I/O
•Concurrency means multiple processes or 
threads executing simultaneously and possibly 
accessing the same file or resource.
•If not handled properly, concurrent access can 
lead to:
•Race conditions
•Data corruption
•Inconsistent output
98

Synchronization Techniques
(a) File Locking
•Locks prevent multiple processes from modifying the 
same file region at once.
(b) Thread Synchronization
•In multi-threaded programs, mutexes are used to 
ensure that only one thread performs file I/O at a 
time.

Process Creation and Control (fork, exec)
In System-Level Programming, a process is a 
program in execution — it includes the program 
code, data, registers, and system resources.
•The Operating System (OS) provides system 
calls to:
–Create new processes
–Control process execution
–Replace process images
100

Two important system calls in UNIX/Linux are:
•fork() → creates a new process
•exec() → replaces the process image with a 
new program
•Together, they form the foundation of process 
management in UNIX-like operating systems.
101

What is a Process?
A process consists of:
–Program Code (Text Segment), 
–Data Segment
–Stack
–Heap
–Program Counter (PC)
–Set of Registers
Each process has a unique Process ID (PID) assigned by 
the OS.
102

Process Creation using fork()
•fork() is a system call used to create a new 
process by duplicating the calling (parent) 
process.
•The newly created process is called the child 
process.
•The original process is called the parent process.
•Both processes execute the same program from 
the next instruction after fork().
103

Replacing Process Image using exec()
•The exec() family of system calls replaces the current 
process image with a new program.
•After exec():
•The current program is completely replaced.
•Execution continues from the new program’s main().
•The PID remains the same, but the program and 
memory are replaced.
Combining fork() and exec()
•A common use is to create a new process using 
fork() and then replace the child’s program using 
exec().
104

channel used for data transfer between 
related processes (such as parent and child).
•It acts as a temporary buffer in the kernel that 
connects the output of one process to the 
input of another.

•Simple to use for process communication

•Ideal for parent–child data sharing

–Ignore the signal, or

Thread Safety
•A function is thread-safe if it can be called 
simultaneously by multiple threads without 
causing conflicts.
To ensure safety:
•Use mutexes or semaphores
•Avoid global variables or shared writable data
•Use thread-local storage if necessary
